agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A new 'auto-split' feature is requested that would automatically divide shipments exceeding 500 kg into multiple sub-shipments, each assigned to a different carrier, bypassing supervisor involvement. This directly conflicts with two established production invariants in the existing domain model.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of freight to be transported, which must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The transport provider assigned to fulfill a Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept: a child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment. Not yet part of the domain model."},{"term":"Auto-split","definition":"Proposed automatic process that divides an overweight Shipment into smaller Sub-shipments, each independently assigned to a Carrier."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role that is the only authority permitted to reassign a Carrier on a Shipment."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment Lock","definition":"The invariant that once a Carrier is assigned, only a Supervisor can change it."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a data consistency and state integrity constraint. A Shipment with zero or more than one Carrier is in a corrupt state — accountability, billing, liability, and tracking all depend on a single Carrier assignment.","testable_assertion":"For every Shipment in any non-draft state, COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization integrity constraint. Bypassing it undermines the supervisory control that exists to prevent unauthorized or erroneous reassignments, which have direct liability and contractual consequences.","testable_assertion":"Any Carrier reassignment event on a Shipment must have an associated actor with the Supervisor role."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment has exactly one Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a physical and operational constraint. A shipment cannot be dispatched without knowing who is carrying it. Dispatch with zero or multiple carriers creates ambiguous custody.","testable_assertion":"The dispatch operation rejects any Shipment where assigned_carrier is null or where multiple carriers are assigned."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A Shipment already has a Carrier assigned and then its weight is updated to exceed 500 kg, triggering auto-split.","expected_behavior":"The existing Carrier assignment would need to be broken or reassigned without Supervisor involvement, violating BR-2."},{"scenario":"A sub-shipment is created but no Carrier is available for it.","expected_behavior":"The sub-shipment would exist with no Carrier, violating BR-1 and BR-3."},{"scenario":"A Shipment is split into sub-shipments mid-transit.","expected_behavior":"Custody chain is broken. The original Carrier's contractual obligation becomes ambiguous. Liability is undefined."},{"scenario":"A parent Shipment is split but the parent entity remains in the system.","expected_behavior":"Does the parent Shipment still have a Carrier? What is its status? The one-Carrier invariant becomes unclear for the parent-child relationship."},{"scenario":"A customer queries tracking for the original Shipment after a split.","expected_behavior":"They now need to track multiple sub-shipments across multiple carriers — a UX and contractual change that affects the customer agreement."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Splitting shipments across multiple carriers changes billing structures, contractual obligations, and liability allocation. Without clear business rules for cost apportionment, revenue leakage or disputes are likely.","operational":"Introduces a fundamentally new domain concept (sub-shipments) that every downstream system — tracking, billing, dispatch, reporting, customer service — must understand. This is not a feature; it is a domain model evolution.","user":"Customers who shipped a single package now receive multiple tracking numbers. Carrier partners who accepted one job now see it fragmented. Both groups experience a breaking change in their mental model.","risk":"HIGH. Two production invariants are directly violated. Deploying this without resolving the contradictions risks corrupt shipment states, accountability gaps, and potential contractual/legal exposure.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Must handle a new entity type (sub-shipment) and new assignment workflows. Supervisory controls are bypassed."},{"group":"Carriers / Partners","impact":"Contractual scope changes — they may be assigned partial loads without negotiation. Existing SLAs may not cover split shipments."},{"group":"Customers / Shippers","impact":"A single shipment becomes multiple. Tracking, insurance, and delivery guarantees are fragmented."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Cost allocation, invoicing, and revenue recognition must account for split shipments. No rules exist for this today."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"Liability for goods in transit becomes ambiguous when custody is split. Contracts likely assume one carrier per shipment."},{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their authority over carrier assignment is bypassed by an automated process, undermining the control structure."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains two direct contradictions with established production invariants: (1) auto-split assigns multiple carriers to what was a single shipment, violating the one-carrier-per-shipment invariant, and (2) it bypasses supervisor authority for carrier assignment, violating the assignment lock invariant. These are not ambiguities — they are clear conflicts. Confidence is low because the feature as specified cannot be built without either breaking production invariants or fundamentally redefining the domain model, and the spec provides no guidance on which path to take.","high_confidence_areas":["The two invariant violations are clear and unambiguous","The feature introduces a new aggregate (sub-shipment) not present in the current model","Downstream impact on billing, tracking, and contracts is certain"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends to retire the existing invariants or work within them","Whether 'sub-shipment' should be a first-class entity or a different aggregate entirely","How liability and contractual obligations should be allocated across sub-shipments","Whether the 500 kg threshold is a hard invariant or a configurable policy"],"assumptions":["The existing domain model and invariants as stated are accurate and currently enforced in production","The product team has not yet approved a domain model evolution to accommodate this feature"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature directly violates BR-1 (one Carrier per Shipment). Splitting a shipment into sub-shipments each with a different carrier means the original business concept of a Shipment having exactly one Carrier is broken. Either the invariant must be retired, or 'sub-shipment' must be modeled as a fully independent Shipment (not a child of the original), each satisfying BR-1 independently.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Carriers","Customers","Finance","Legal"],"options":["A: Model sub-shipments as independent first-class Shipments, each with exactly one Carrier, preserving BR-1. The parent becomes a 'Shipment Group' or 'Order' — a new aggregate.","B: Relax BR-1 to allow multiple Carriers per Shipment via a new 'multi-carrier shipment' concept. This is a fundamental domain model change requiring stakeholder sign-off.","C: Reject the feature as incompatible with the current domain model."],"recommendation":"Option A. It preserves existing invariants by introducing a new aggregate (e.g., ShipmentGroup) that contains multiple independent Shipments. Each sub-shipment is a real Shipment with one Carrier. This is a domain evolution, not a violation."},{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature explicitly bypasses Supervisor authority for carrier assignment (BR-2). The request states 'without requiring supervisor involvement,' which directly contradicts the invariant that only a Supervisor can reassign carriers. If sub-shipments are new Shipments (not reassignments), this may be resolvable — but only under Option A above.","affected_stakeholders":["Supervisors","Operations","Compliance"],"options":["A: If sub-shipments are new independent Shipments, initial carrier assignment (not reassignment) does not require Supervisor involvement — BR-2 is preserved because no reassignment occurs.","B: Treat auto-split as a system-level Supervisor action (the system acts with Supervisor authority). This weakens the invariant.","C: Require Supervisor approval for auto-split, contradicting the feature request but preserving the invariant."],"recommendation":"Option A. If each sub-shipment is a new Shipment receiving its first carrier assignment, BR-2 is not violated because there is no reassignment. The original Shipment is cancelled or transitioned to a 'split' terminal state. This requires the product team to accept that auto-split creates new Shipments, not modifies the existing one."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple carriers to a single shipment's freight","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier"},{"violation":"Auto-split bypasses supervisor authority for carrier changes","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier on a Shipment"},{"violation":"Feature introduces 'sub-shipment' concept with no domain model definition, lifecycle, or relationship semantics","business_rule_broken":"Domain model integrity — new aggregate introduced without bounded context analysis"}]}
